Title: A transient expression tool box for anthocyanin biosynthesis in Nicotiana benthamiana

Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: CRISPR mutagenesis in N. benthamiana; transient anthocyanin pathway induction
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in leaves and strongly induced by Rosea1; developmentally regulated contribution inferred
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR knockout line Nb 29-2; transient expression assays; LC-MS loss of rutinosides
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in leaves according to ATLAS
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Knockout required maintenance of specialized line; RNAi silencing was incomplete whereas CRISPR was complete
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Knocking out RhamT is highly valuable for producing pure anthocyanin 3-O-glucosides and enabling complex anthocyanin engineering.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core host-interference genes; elimination of both homologues abolished rutinoside formation completely.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Endogenous RhamT genes converted basic anthocyanin 3-O-glucosides to rutinosides in wildtype N. benthamiana. 2. CRISPR knockout of both homologues in line Nb 29-2 eliminated rutinoside formation completely. 3. This enabled production of pure glucosides and reconstruction of complex anthocyanins such as A5.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of RhamT shifts products from rutinosides to pure 3-O-glucosides

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not required when transiently expressed in this system
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Not necessary for anthocyanin production in this assay
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not a priority component for this transient anthocyanin chassis
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Tested because of known role in proanthocyanidin precursor transport, but apparently unnecessary here.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TT12/AP was tested as a potential anthocyanin transport component. 2. It was not necessary for anthocyanin accumulation in the N. benthamiana leaf system. 3. Endogenous transport capacity and/or GST function were sufficient without it.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: No clear requirement for anthocyanin accumulation in this system

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: line Nb 29-2 background for CRISPR tests
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Rosea1 induction; RNAi silencing hairpin pAGM81963; CRISPR knockout
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by Rosea1, not evidently pre-expressed before induction
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR knockout screening; RNAi silencing; metabolite profiling with Rosea1+ANS combinations
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced in infiltrated leaves by Rosea1
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: CRISPR mutant plants were sterile; RNAi silencing was more practical and sufficient
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Transient silencing is a practical way to suppress endogenous delphinidin bias and permit pelargonidin/cyanidin production.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Host-interference genes rather than heterologous targets; removal required for non-delphinidin products in TF-driven system.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Endogenous F3'5'H homologues induced by Rosea1 bias metabolism toward delphinidin. 2. Their suppression was necessary to obtain pelargonidin and cyanidin products in the TF-based system. 3. Complete CRISPR knockout caused sterility, whereas hairpin RNAi provided practical transient suppression.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Suppression redirects flux away from delphinidin toward pelargonidin/cyanidin

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Endogenous N. benthamiana ANS activity was limiting for anthocyanin production induced by transcription factors. 2. Co-expression of heterologous Arabidopsis ANS with Rosea1 converted weak anthocyanin output into high-level delphinidin glycoside production. 3. In the RhamT-knockout line Nb 29-2, Rosea1+AtANS enabled high-level production of pure delphinidin 3-O-glucoside.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Endogenous ANS activity detectable at low level after TF induction; insufficient in leaves of the standard lab strain for robust anthocyanin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: delphinidin 3-O-glucoside; delphinidin 3-O-rutinoside; cyanidin-based anthocyanins when paired with appropriate upstream genes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper suggests one endogenous N. benthamiana ANS homologue may contain a frameshift and/or low expression in the lab strain; heterologous ANS bypasses this bottleneck.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient co-expression with Rosea1 in wildtype and Nb 29-2 leaves; LC-MS metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration in greenhouse-grown leaves; comparison of wildtype vs Nb 29-2 background
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key bottleneck-bypass gene for robust transient anthocyanin engineering in N. benthamiana; useful as a fixed transgene or stack component.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms anthocyanidin precursors of target anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a bottleneck/key control step in this study for TF-driven production
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strongly promotes high-level anthocyanin accumulation; rescues poor TF-only production in N. benthamiana leaves

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Heterologous F3'5'H enabled production of delphinidin glycosides in N. benthamiana. 2. Petunia F3'5'H produced mixed pelargonidin, cyanidin and delphinidin derivatives, indicating broader substrate/product outcome. 3. Campanula F3'5'H gave predominantly delphinidin derivatives and was preferable for selective D3G/D3R production.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Endogenous N. benthamiana F3'5'H genes are induced by Rosea1; their suppression is needed for non-delphinidin products
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Knockout of endogenous N. benthamiana F3'5'H homologues caused sterility in tested CRISPR plants; silencing is more practical
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Petunia enzyme was less selective; Campanula enzyme was more selective for delphinidin branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression substitution experiments; LC-MS profiling of anthocyanin products
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration; wildtype vs Nb 29-2 background; RNAi silencing of endogenous F3'5'H
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Preferred branch-control enzyme for engineering delphinidin-rich anthocyanins; Campanula allele offers higher product selectivity.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Determines branch flux toward delphinidin products
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates flavonoid B-ring at 3' and 5' positions to generate delphinidin-branch precursors
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes delphinidin-type anthocyanin accumulation and novel delphinidin A5 formation

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis F3'H was required for efficient cyanidin 3-O-glucoside production because endogenous N. benthamiana leaves lacked sufficient activity. 2. Omitting F3'H redirected flux to pelargonidin glycosides. 3. Co-expression with Rosea1, ANS and DFR enabled robust C3G production in Nb 29-2.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not detectably or sufficiently expressed endogenously in infiltrated leaves for C3G production
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts as branch determinant: absence yields pelargonidin branch; presence yields cyanidin branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Omission/addition transient expression tests; LC-MS metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration; wildtype vs Nb 29-2 background
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful branch-selection gene to switch between pelargonidin and cyanidin products in transient engineering.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Determines branch flux toward cyanidin products
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates flavonoid B-ring at the 3' position to produce cyanidin-branch precursors
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes cyanidin-type anthocyanin accumulation; omission causes pelargonidin accumulation

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DFR was one of the absolutely required genes for anthocyanin production in the pathway-only reconstruction. 2. Arabidopsis DFR supported production of all three basic anthocyanin glycosides. 3. Tomato DFR produced pelargonidin less efficiently, and N. benthamiana endogenous DFR was inferred to poorly use dihydrokaempferol; Pelargonium DFR restored pelargonidin 3-O-glucoside production under F3'5'H silencing.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Pelargonium zonale; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Pelargonium zonale; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Endogenous DFR activity in N. benthamiana appears inadequate for efficient pelargonidin formation from dihydrokaempferol
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: pelargonidin 3-O-glucoside; cyanidin 3-O-glucoside; delphinidin 3-O-glucoside and corresponding derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Species-specific substrate preference was a critical determinant of which anthocyanidin branch accumulated.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene omission tests; allele comparison among Arabidopsis, tomato and Pelargonium; LC-MS profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration; with or without F3'5'H silencing
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Choice of DFR allele is essential for tailoring anthocyanin branch output, especially pelargonidin engineering.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor pool for ANS
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces dihydroflavonols to leucoanthocyanidins using NADPH
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step via allele-specific substrate preference
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls efficiency of pelargonidin/cyanidin/delphinidin formation depending on enzyme source

Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: AT5G17220; petunia GST cloned in study
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GST was absolutely required for anthocyanin accumulation in the pathway-only reconstruction. 2. Petunia GST and Arabidopsis TT19 gave comparable anthocyanin accumulation. 3. GST enabled vacuolar sequestration needed for visible high-level anthocyanin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Endogenous activity insufficient in this reconstruction context
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although GST is not a biosynthetic enzyme, it was experimentally essential for anthocyanin accumulation; classified here as pathway-supporting transport/sequestration factor.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene omission tests and alternative ortholog comparison; LC-MS profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important support gene to include in transient/stable anthocyanin engineering stacks.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/sequestration of target product
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Binds anthocyanins or anthocyanin intermediates to facilitate vacuolar sequestration
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Necessary for anthocyanin accumulation; omission abolished detectable anthocyanin

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGT79B1 was one of four Arabidopsis genes required to reconstruct anthocyanin A5 biosynthesis in N. benthamiana. 2. Co-expression with Rosea1, ANS, F3'H, DFR, At5GT, At3AT1 and At5MAT yielded strong accumulation of A5. 3. The same decoration module also enabled pelargonidin A5 and delphinidin A5 synthesis when the aglycone branch was changed.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: anthocyanin A5; pelargonidin A5; delphinidin A5
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functions in decoration rather than core aglycone formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial transient expression and LC-MS detection of A5-mass peaks
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration in Nb 29-2 line
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: xylosylated anthocyanin intermediate in A5 pathway
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for building complex and novel decorated anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for modification of target product scaffold
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Adds xylose to anthocyanin glucoside acceptor during A5 assembly
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required component for A5 and A5-derivative accumulation

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. At5GT was required in the four-gene Arabidopsis module for A5 formation in N. benthamiana. 2. Together with UGT79B1, At3AT1 and At5MAT, it enabled high-level accumulation of A5. 3. This module functioned on cyanidin, pelargonidin and delphinidin backbones.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: anthocyanin A5; pelargonidin A5; delphinidin A5
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Late decoration gene in Arabidopsis anthocyanin pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial transient expression and LC-MS detection
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration in Nb 29-2 line
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Enables construction of multiply glycosylated anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for modification of target product scaffold
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose to the 5-O position of anthocyanidin-derived acceptors

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. At3AT1 was part of the four-gene module necessary for A5 biosynthesis in N. benthamiana. 2. Co-expression produced A5 peaks consistent with coumaroylated anthocyanin. 3. The module also decorated pelargonidin and delphinidin backbones to form novel A5 variants.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: anthocyanin A5; pelargonidin A5; delphinidin A5
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Peak pattern indicated likely cis/trans coumaroyl isomers of A5.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial transient expression and LC-MS detection of A5-mass peaks
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration in Nb 29-2 line
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful acylation gene for engineering structurally diverse anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for modification of target product scaffold
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers p-coumaroyl group onto anthocyanin glucoside during A5 assembly
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for A5 and A5-derivative production

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. At5MAT was part of the four-gene Arabidopsis module sufficient for high-level A5 biosynthesis in N. benthamiana. 2. It supported formation of cyanidin A5 and corresponding pelargonidin/delphinidin A5 analogs. 3. This demonstrated that complex anthocyanin acylation modules can be ported into N. benthamiana.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: anthocyanin A5; pelargonidin A5; delphinidin A5
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Required A5 decoration enzyme in the transferred Arabidopsis module.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial transient expression and LC-MS detection
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration in Nb 29-2 line
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supports engineering of stable and highly decorated anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for modification of target product scaffold
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers malonyl group onto 5-O-glucosylated anthocyanin intermediate in A5 biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for A5 and A5-derivative accumulation

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Addition of AtBGLU10 to the A5-producing module generated a peak consistent with anthocyanin A8. 2. A8 accumulated only at very low level, with A5 remaining the major product. 3. Low activity was discussed as likely due to suboptimal donor/substrate context in N. benthamiana.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Very low A8 yield in N. benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Likely limited by donor availability and vacuolar context; enzyme normally uses acyl-glucose donors such as sinapoylglucose.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Addition of BGLU10 to A5 module and LC-MS detection of m/z 1137 peak
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration in Nb 29-2 line
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Shows feasibility but also donor-limitation caveat for terminal glycosylation engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Adds an additional glucose to A5-type anthocyanin using an acyl-glucose donor

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Addition of AtSAT to the A5/BGLU10 module failed to produce the expected A11 anthocyanin in N. benthamiana. 2. The negative result indicated that sinapoylglucose donor required by SAT is likely absent in N. benthamiana. 3. This highlighted host precursor limitations for reconstruction of complex anthocyanin pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No detectable A11 production due to missing specialized donor substrate in host
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core negative evidence for pathway limitation by host metabolite availability.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Addition of SAT to A5/BGLU10 module and LC-MS showing no A11 peak
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration in Nb 29-2 line
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: A9/A11-type sinapoylated anthocyanin expected but not detected
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Indicates need to co-engineer precursor supply, especially sinapoylglucose, for successful A11 production.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product but unsupported in host context
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Would transfer sinapoyl group from sinapoylglucose to A5-type anthocyanin to form A9/A11-related products
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Did not increase A11; reveals missing donor limitation

Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Expression alone or with Delila sometimes caused necrosis; induces endogenous genes including undesired RhamT and F3'5'H activities
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin biosynthetic pathway genes; endogenous N. benthamiana F3'5'H and RhamT genes are induced in the applied system
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Transient heterologous expression by Agrobacterium infiltration
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Rosea1 alone induced only low-level delphinidin 3-O-rutinoside accumulation in wildtype N. benthamiana. 2. Rosea1 became a robust high-output activator when combined with heterologous ANS. 3. Together with selected pathway genes and silencing of endogenous F3'5'H, Rosea1 enabled reproducible production of P3G, C3G, D3G and complex anthocyanins.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient Agrobacterium infiltration; wildtype vs Nb 29-2 background; RNAi silencing of endogenous F3'5'H
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Central activator for compact transient engineering schemes; best used with heterologous ANS and context-specific pathway genes/silencing cassettes.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not endogenous; transiently expressed in infiltrated leaves
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression alone and with pathway genes; visible pigmentation and LC-MS
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: pelargonidin 3-O-glucoside; cyanidin 3-O-glucoside; delphinidin 3-O-glucoside; A5 and derivatives
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Master activator enabling robust anthocyanin accumulation when paired with limiting biosynthetic functions
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller; strong activator whose output depends on complementation of limiting downstream steps and removal of competing host activities
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Preferred over PAP1 and Delila alone for robust engineering in this study.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates endogenous anthocyanin pathway genes and also undesired endogenous F3'5'H and RhamT activities
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions with bHLH Delila in MBW-like regulatory context

Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Only low-level D3R production in N. benthamiana leaves
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Transient heterologous expression by Agrobacterium infiltration
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAP1 expression alone induced only weak anthocyanin biosynthesis in N. benthamiana. 2. The product was limited to delphinidin 3-O-rutinoside. 3. PAP1 was less useful than the Rosea1-based system for high-level anthocyanin engineering in this paper.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Lower-priority activator in this chassis compared with Rosea1
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Not decisive for high-level production in this study
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as comparator TF rather than main engineering solution.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates endogenous anthocyanin genes insufficiently for high output

Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Alone inactive; with Rosea1 or PAP1 could cause necrosis in some leaves
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin biosynthetic genes in combination with MYB partner
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Transient heterologous expression by Agrobacterium infiltration
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Delila alone did not induce anthocyanin biosynthesis in N. benthamiana. 2. Co-expression with Rosea1 or PAP1 still yielded only low-level D3R. 3. Delila was not required for the optimized Rosea1-plus-pathway-gene toolbox developed here.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not favored in this N. benthamiana engineering system because of low yield and occasional necrosis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression alone and with MYB TFs; visible phenotype and LC-MS
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: No effect alone; weak enhancement context with MYBs
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Served mainly as a comparison to prior MBW-based activation approaches.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Co-activates anthocyanin genes with MYB partner
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions in MBW-like complex with MYB/WDR partners
